Finding: Arithmetic/Notation.lean
Static observation: 5095 lines, 903 declarations, 0 notation/syntax/macro declarations by static grep.
Risk: The notation module is an alias/plan-semantics monolith; Part 2 must not import it as an API.
Refactoring consequence: Split into true Notation/CoreSymbols, Legacy/PhaseAliases, and NeutralWrappers.

Finding: Plan-semantic Smoke names
Static observation: Many active derivation sites carry SM/R/Recheck/Plus/Full/Audit in file names, e.g. SM9h, SM9e, R3c1.
Risk: Without Plan Part 1, the code surface is not readable by domain role.
Refactoring consequence: New public modules must be object/role based only; old modules remain legacy sources.

Finding: Operational SM9h core
Static observation: SM9h leaves an ExecComplex discriminant derived from a quadratic window, but under historical names.
Risk: Mathematics is usable, but not Part-2-neutral.
Refactoring consequence: First wrap Operational/QuadraticWindow and Operational/QuadraticDiscriminant.

Finding: Orders / number theory
Static observation: Discriminant, quadratic order, norm forms and Galois/Eisenstein special objects exist in bridge form.
Risk: Still no general D_op -> D_fund/conductor/order pipeline.
Refactoring consequence: Build NumberTheory/* only after the operational profile is neutralized.

Finding: Cayley-Dickson
Static observation: The Structural/CayleyDickson path includes star, normSq, multiplication, alternative-law and norm-multiplicativity material.
Risk: Do not delete; no free identification with sectors.
Refactoring consequence: Register it as a structural consumer/bridge and keep it out of the arithmetic source chain until a bridge is proved.

Finding: Handoff
Static observation: A_to_B exports carrier, Schur, regionNet, split, DtN, selectedLevel, and regime data. Inputs B/C/D/E_to_A exist as empty/small structures.
Risk: Pillar A could accidentally treat its own outputs as sources.
Refactoring consequence: The access matrix enforces: A consumes only inputs; outputs are forbidden-to-A.

Finding: Finite/AQFT seeds
Static observation: StateSpace, MatrixExponential, GibbsStateSeed, UnitaryEvolution, and ChannelSeed exist as A-internal seeds.
Risk: They can behave like parallel physics generators if not attached to an A-operator profile.
Refactoring consequence: Mark them as consumers of BoundaryOperator/ExecMatrix; no independent operator source.

Finding: Implementation preparation must be proof-rehearsed
Static observation: Previous SM phases often exposed missing sources only during implementation.
Risk: The assistant may patch consumers, obstruct too early, or forget ExecComplex/import/no-go constraints.
Refactoring consequence: Every phase record has preparation fields: dependency audit, source-derived theory objects, proof rehearsal, tactic plan, natural fallback source, and explicit no-go checklist.

Finding: Integrated Lean module inventory
Static observation: The SM9h snapshot contains 287 Lean modules, 724 internal import edges, zero missing internal imports, and an acyclic import graph according to build_export_script_v1.8.py.
Risk: If module names and dependency structure are not visible in the planning tool, object/phase planning can drift away from the repository surface.
Refactoring consequence: The planning package now carries a generated module catalog, dependency summaries, source-path links, and graph figures derived from the repository snapshot. These are generated views, not manual planning sources.

Finding: TomS thread cross-check / external-reference intake
Static observation: Thread pages 1-5 introduce prime-generation heuristics, imaginary-quadratic lattices, SL2Z/j, L-functions via modular forms, finite simple groups, Monster/Moonshine, Leech lattice and VOA/string-theory context.
Risk: These topics are established-reference material. They must not become CNNA generators, hidden axioms, imported physical ontology, or proof shortcuts; several statements require precision corrections before formal use.
Refactoring consequence: Register the material as source-audit and comparison-only objects unless a later phase derives the required CNNA object from A provenance. Add Leech/finite-simple-group/VOA/Moonshine comparison profiles after j/q-expansion, not before.

Finding: Phase 1.1 split refinement / Legacy.StatusGates quarantine
Static observation: The next frontier combines three different work kinds: declaration classification, legacy enum quarantine, true notation extraction, and import firewall verification.
Risk: Keeping this as one implementation phase would recreate the previous monolith problem and make missing imports look like proof failures.
Refactoring consequence: Split 1.1 into 1.1.1-1.1.5. Activate only 1.1.1 first; move the 749 single-constructor status enums in 1.1.2, not during inventory.

Finding: Declaration-level consumption gap
Static observation: Module graph reachability is strong, but declaration-level semantic consumption is not equivalent to BuildAll/Umbrella import reachability. Current snapshot: 287 Lean modules, 724 internal import edges, 8860 declarations, 394 static reference edges, 1686 referenced symbols.
Risk: A declaration can look consumed because an umbrella imports its module, while it is only a status/audit marker or phase alias. Blind pruning or blind retention would both corrupt the derived-only path.
Refactoring consequence: Insert Phase 1.1.1.1 and LEG5 before code movement: classify declarations as core-computational, proof-bridge, public-wrapper, legacy-status, legacy-policy, phase-alias, audit-only, blue-semantic-correction, dead-candidate, or final-output.

Finding: Single-constructor inductives are broader than status enums
Static observation: A rough direct static scan finds about 872 single-constructor inductives in 99 modules; the earlier 749 figure should be treated as a narrower status-enum subset, not the full population.
Risk: Moving every single-constructor inductive into Legacy.StatusGates would swallow policy/proof-bridge declarations and hide real provenance.
Refactoring consequence: Phase 1.1.2 must consume the classification from 1.1.1.1/1.1.1.3. Constructor count alone is not a quarantine criterion.

Finding: Blue objects are proof-carrying but not public-final
Static observation: Object status B rows occur in Cayley-Dickson, Schur/DtN, and operational arithmetic families. These are already derived-only/proof-carrying in current code, but still semantically/API unfinished.
Risk: Treating blue objects as final because proofs exist would export old SM/AR semantics or wrong API boundaries into Phase 3+ and handoffs.
Refactoring consequence: Add Phase 1.1.1.2 and LEG7. Every blue object must get a correction ledger entry: semantic defect, natural correction phase, allowed temporary consumers, forbidden public consumers, and exit-to-white condition.

Finding: Pruning must follow API contracts, not precede them
Static observation: Syntactic sinks and weakly referenced declarations include diagnostic leaves, audit leaves, intentional outputs, and proof-obligation terminals.
Risk: Deleting before a public API contract can remove audit/provenance evidence or proof bridges that are not mainline outputs but still necessary.
Refactoring consequence: Add Phase 1.1.1.4 and LEG8. Deletion is postponed to a later optional pruning phase and requires classification plus local green build evidence.

Finding: Module budget missing before implementation
Static observation: The v23 plan classifies module/declaration consumption but did not yet require every implementation phase to name the exact modules to add/modify before Lean code is written.
Risk: Kleinteilige phases can degenerate into kleinteilige code: one new file per subphase, accidental wrappers, and parallel helper modules that are syntactically green but semantically fragmented.
Refactoring consequence: Insert 1.1.1.1.1 and LEG10. Every implementation phase must carry a module manifest with exact module names/count, semantic placement rationale, reuse decision, and import role before code starts.

Finding: Duplicate generator risk
Static observation: The Smoke-Test Generator is intended to become the canonical generator seam for the whole theory, but later phases could accidentally reimplement request/output, extraction, ledger, or smoke-test logic under new names.
Risk: Duplicate generator paths would break derived-only provenance by creating competing sources of truth and would make future proof reuse fragile.
Refactoring consequence: Insert 1.1.1.1.2 and LEG11. Generator-like code must reuse or extend the canonical generator at its natural layer; parallel generators are blocked unless a genuine semantic split is recorded.

Finding: Secondary derivation-graph analysis assets exist
Static observation: repo_inventory/raw_export already contains import graph data and related forensics sufficient for future documentation coverage and derivation-geometry diagnostics.
Risk: If activated too early, graph metrics could be mistaken for CNNA-generative evidence or distract from closing operational derived objects.
Refactoring consequence: Keep as secondary long-term phases 19.1-19.4; use for documentation/audit only until core derived objects and public API are stable.

Finding: ExecComplex rational ceiling
Static observation: ExecComplex is represented as rational pairs Q x Q; later CM/lattice objects require algebraic or analytic data.
Risk: Operational path may become a skeleton or silently import mirror/noncomputable semantics.
Refactoring consequence: Adopt EXEC-2: operational code carries rational/integer invariants; algebraic irrational data remains mirror-only unless a return invariant is proved.

Finding: Refactor regression risk
Static observation: Notation/alias cleanup touches a large declaration and import surface.
Risk: A single large move can create ambiguous red states and consumer-side patches.
Refactoring consequence: Use wrapper-first bounded migrations with declaration-count, import-delta, consumer-breakage, and local full-build evidence.

Finding: IdealThread singleton collapse is code-backed
Static observation: ToC/Contract.lean defines IdealThread.carrier L as {U.cell L}; IdealThread.family uses that carrier; deterministic coarsen_carrier_eq_prev proves coarsenSet of the next carrier is the previous singleton.
Risk: Any plan that uses an existing IdealThread route as a nontrivial boundary matrix source would silently collapse to scalar/degenerate data.
Refactoring consequence: Add S07 and BR06. Later full-boundary/DtN phases must explicitly reject IdealThread as full matrix source unless a separate lifting theorem is supplied.

Finding: CutSpec.full is the maximal carrier route
Static observation: Finite/CutSpec.lean defines full T with carrier := T.carrier and full_carrier is rfl.
Risk: Confusing CutSpec.full with IdealThread or with a diagonal/scalar route would erase the intended sector/interior/interface geometry.
Refactoring consequence: Add S08 and require Phase 3.0 boundary-route separation before neutral Schur/DtN wrappers.

Finding: Exact quadratic scalar provenance gap
Static observation: v35 treated O09 as optional/pilot; CM/lattice phases will require exact quadratic coordinates, but derived-only prohibits free sqrt(D).
Risk: A scalar consumer could silently introduce theta/sqrt(D) without an operational discriminant source, turning a derived-only construction into an axiom or mirror import.
Refactoring consequence: v36 requires O09 as schema, adds O10/BR14 as D-source instantiation bridge, and adds GNG19 no-free-sqrt enforcement.

Addressing phase: all phases before implementation; immediate enforcement before 1.1.1.2
Finding: Static finding lifecycle fields were missing
Static observation: The Section 2 Static code findings table listed risks and consequences but did not force an issue ID, current status, priority color, or addressing phase.
Risk: A known finding could be re-discovered in later work, silently left open, or closed by an unrelated phase without a visible provenance link.
Refactoring consequence: Every static finding row now requires ID, Priority, Issue status, and Addressing phase. New findings must be added to Section 2 before implementation continues.

Addressing phase: all active phases; current gate before 1.1.1.2
Finding: Pre-start feasibility check was implicit, not a hard visible workflow gate
Static observation: The scratchpad contained dependency audit/proof rehearsal fields, but the workflow table did not require a separate go/no-go feasibility decision immediately before starting the next phase.
Risk: A phase could begin from a green prior build while a static finding or missing dependency still requires a natural backjump, producing consumer-side patches or over-fragmented governance work.
Refactoring consequence: A mandatory pre-start feasibility workflow now checks active phase dependencies, static findings, module manifest, reuse audit, and whether the phase is infrastructure-only or code-changing before implementation starts.

Finding: Blue correction cannot rely only on status B labels
Static observation: The active Phase 1.1.1.2 consumes object register rows with Object status B, but a valid LEG7 ledger must also bind each row to proof dossier, current derivation site, allowed temporary consumers, forbidden consumers, and exit-to-white condition.
Risk: A proof-carrying blue object could be whitened or publicly wrapped merely because the object status row was found, without recording the exact semantic/API defect and correction phase.
Refactoring consequence: Phase 1.1.1.2 must generate or validate a per-blue-object correction ledger, not a status-only summary. Public API and pruning phases remain blocked until that ledger is closed.

Finding: Generated table views needed local, section-specific legends.
Static observation: Earlier rendering either lacked local legends or inserted an over-broad mixed legend. The current generator emits static/phase/object legends only where the corresponding colored cells exist.
Risk: Wrong or missing legend text makes color semantics ambiguous across static findings, object status, and phase status tables.
Refactoring consequence: Use explicit legend kinds in the generator and no legend for non-status tables.

Finding: Tool contract version and data snapshot version were conflated as v40-v44 document increments.
Static observation: The previous split still allowed the Lean-code data timestamp to move when only the generator/PDF display changed.
Risk: A documentation-only edit could be misread as a Lean-code data change, destroying reproducible alignment between Lean snapshots and planning artifacts.
Refactoring consequence: Metadata now separates tool_version, tool_change_timestamp, and data_timestamp. tool_version increments by 0.01 for tool/display changes; data_timestamp is restored and changes only with Lean code/data.

Finding: Essential findings front matter duplicated issue tracking outside Section 2.
Static observation: The v44 PDF opened with prose bullets that contained actionable workflow findings, while Section 2 was supposed to be the issue ledger.
Risk: Important problems can remain unprioritized, unassigned, or forgotten if they live outside the Static code findings table.
Refactoring consequence: The separate Essential findings section was removed; the actionable content is represented as SCF rows with priority, lifecycle status, and addressing assignment.

Finding: Complete Lean module catalog: Module column could run visually into the Group column.
Static observation: Long dotted Lean module names were emitted inside raw href cells without dot-level breakpoints and with too little Module-column width.
Risk: The generated module inventory becomes unreliable as a review object if readers cannot distinguish module names from group labels.
Refactoring consequence: Module and path link text is now breakable at dots; the Module column is widened and the Group/Path widths are adjusted.

Finding: Object table A IDs were row anchors but not clickable links.
Static observation: object_table_id_cell(target=True) emitted a hypertarget around plain ID text, while other object tables emitted hyperlinks to that row.
Risk: The first object table looked less navigable than the rest of the proofbook workflow and did not expose the proof-dossier jump from the canonical object list.
Refactoring consequence: Object table A IDs now remain row anchors and additionally link to the corresponding proof-dossier target.

Finding: Global table legends were semantically over-broad.
Static observation: The V1.0 generator inserted the same mixed object/phase/static legend before generated tables even when the table had no colored status cells or the section required a narrower legend.
Risk: A reader could interpret colors with the wrong status system, especially static findings and phase rows.
Refactoring consequence: The generator now supports explicit legend kinds (static, phase, object) and emits no legend for tables without colored status/triage semantics.

Finding: Lean-code data timestamp advanced during a display-only update.
Static observation: The V1.0 legend-policy update reported a new data timestamp even though no Lean source file was changed.
Risk: Downstream reviewers could infer a new Lean data state from a purely presentational PDF/generator change.
Refactoring consequence: data_timestamp/data_version are reset to 2026-05-10T05:51:42+02:00; tool_change_timestamp records the current display-policy correction separately.

Finding: Changed static findings, scratchpad records, and object rows had no visible per-record version/timestamp field.
Static observation: Before V1.01, table readers could see final status but not which tool/display version last changed the row.
Risk: Review history becomes ambiguous when a table row changes without visible version/time metadata.
Refactoring consequence: Static findings expose Change version and Change timestamp. Scratchpad and object proof-link views render record metadata and default unchanged rows to unchanged-by-V1.01 / restored Lean data timestamp.

Finding: PDF navigation lacked a clickable contents page and explicit outline/bookmark tree.
Static observation: Before V1.02, the PDF body started directly with Latest change overview and relied mainly on reader search or section scrolling. The existing hyperref setup did not expose a document-level contents page or an explicitly configured bookmark/outline tree as a reviewed navigation feature.
Risk: Long generated planning PDFs become hard to review, especially when checking sections such as Static code findings, object tables, phase tables, and scratchpad records after tool-only changes.
Refactoring consequence: The PDF preamble now configures section-level PDF bookmarks/outline support, and the document body emits a clickable table of contents immediately after the title. This is a tool/display change only and does not advance the Lean-code data timestamp.

Finding: Forward-looking semantic phase feasibility checks lacked an explicit workflow contract.
Static observation: The plan had per-phase preparation fields and a pre-start feasibility rule, but no dedicated workflow for simulating several semantically connected future phases before implementation and classifying them as implementable or dependency-blocked.
Risk: Without this workflow, missing dependencies can be discovered only during implementation; red findings may not automatically generate upstream addressing phases, causing late backjumps and ad hoc patching pressure.
Refactoring consequence: Tool V1.03 introduces a Pre-Implementation Phase Check: inspect a semantic window as a thought experiment, mark phases Y when implementable and R when dependencies are missing, record new findings, and generate upstream addressing phases for every red result before the blocked phase can become active.

Finding: The tool lacked an explicit phase roadmap for scalable CNNA object-world growth without overloading Lean implementation work.
Static observation: Previous workflows defined phase checks and ledgers, but did not yet separate lean-focus packets, local module-theory context, large end-theory comparison ledgers, incremental cache, object graph slices, and automatic red-blocker prephases into implementation phases.
Risk: Without this roadmap, future requests could drift into manual PDF/table maintenance, excessive context scanning, or ambiguous treatment of established theories during Lean-code work.
Refactoring consequence: Tool V1.04 adds future phases under 1.1.1.5 and a generated implementation-scaling plan. These phases preserve the active 1.1.1.2 cursor and keep Lean-data timestamp fixed.

Finding: Requested semantic phase-window implementability checks had no persistent result table.
Static observation: Tool V1.03 defined the pre-implementation thought-experiment workflow, but the concrete result of a requested check window was not yet rendered as a dedicated generated ledger with phase-level Y/R decisions.
Risk: Feasibility conclusions could remain only in chat prose, making later phase activation ambiguous and weakening the red-blocker-to-addressing-phase discipline.
Refactoring consequence: Tool V1.05 adds a pre_implementation_phase_check_results section, generator validation, TSV export, and PDF rendering for the checked window. The 1.1.1.2-1.1.1.5 window is recorded as yellow/implementable under sequential closure assumptions, with no red addressing phase generated.

Finding: Pre-implementation check status Y was too coarse and conflated active implementability with later implementability in a derivation chain.
Static observation: The V1.05 check correctly treated 1.1.1.3-1.1.1.5 as implementable only under predecessor-closure assumptions, but the table still marked every implementable row yellow.
Risk: Readers could mistake later chain-dependent phases for immediately active implementable phases, weakening the one-active-phase invariant and obscuring predecessor assumptions.
Refactoring consequence: Tool V1.06 introduces G/Y/O/R check semantics plus Check mode, Implementability scope, Required predecessor closures, Assumptions, Evidence basis, Lean proof risk, Parent/subphase rule, and Would-generate-addressing-phase fields.

Finding: Tables that use phase logic needed legends synchronized with the new orange derivation-chain implementability status.
Static observation: The prior phase and pre-implementation legends exposed only green/white/yellow/red or yellow/red meanings and did not explain the new chain-dependent implementability state.
Risk: Even with correct result rows, inconsistent legends would make the PDF display ambiguous and could lead to wrong activation of a non-current phase.
Refactoring consequence: The generator now emits updated phase-logic legends: G = closed/derived-only, W = not yet checked where actual roadmap statuses appear, O = implementable in derivation chain, Y = active implementable now, R = blocked with missing dependencies and mandatory upstream addressing phase.

Finding: Database-ready review/documentation structure was only a prose idea, not a first-class main side objective.
Static observation: The prior YAML had secondary long-term goals and generated views, but no main phase route for database-ready review structure, round-trip schema discipline, and expert-review semantics.
Risk: The DB direction could be treated as optional late tooling and fail to constrain current YAML/schema/review practices.
Refactoring consequence: Add Phase 20 DB-REVIEW with child phases, direct DBR objects, scratchpads, secondary-goal row, workflow row, and immediate guardrails. No Lean source changes.

Finding: Generated documentation lacked an explicit global no-infallibility and discipline-specific expert-review rule.
Static observation: The tool distinguished Lean code from generated views, but did not yet force the stronger epistemic maxim that even green Lean builds and generated artifacts remain semantically reviewable.
Risk: Planning tables or green builds could be overread as 100-percent secured truth, especially around established-theory/reference material that may still lack blue marking.
Refactoring consequence: Add GNG23 and the DB-REVIEW workflow: Lean code is primary but not semantically infallible; generated artifacts are secondary; expert review is always required; missing blue markings are audit debt.

Finding: New substantive Lean code should wait until semantically related module families have been audited for consolidation or explicit no-merge status.
Static observation: The repository has a valid import graph, but it still contains visibly related micro-modules and clone-like wrappers. Existing phases covered reuse/generator policy and declaration consumption, but not a dedicated pre-substantive module-consolidation/no-merge ledger.
Risk: Without this gate, new code may be built on duplicate or unstable import surfaces, making later public API correction, blue-object review, and refactor migration harder.
Refactoring consequence: V1.21 routes this finding through the full readiness chain: source intake, neutral naming/facade plan, refactor execution, post-refactor re-scan, and release proof before 1.1.1.2.

Finding: High-confidence schema/clone families were found in BoundarySource substrate ledgers and Handoff input/output wrappers.
Static observation: BoundaryStateSubstrate, BranchAddressSubstrate, HistoryExpandedSubstrate, and InterfaceExposureSubstrate share the same import, ledger projection pattern, blocker/status/obligation theorems, and StrengtheningAR2b1 wrappers. A_to_C, A_to_D, A_to_E are SectorExportStrong aliases with identical of/export projections. B_to_A, C_to_A, D_to_A, E_to_A share the same source/feedback structure, ext proof, and handoff projection.
Risk: Leaving these as unrelated modules can obscure the true shared schema and make future public API/firewall decisions noisier; merging them unsafely could still break existing imports or erase pillar-specific names.
Refactoring consequence: After LEG37 closes, LEG38 must decide schema/facade/no-merge treatment; any actual code change moves through LEG39 with old-module facades and local build evidence.

Finding: Long SM/generated proof chains and Cayley-Dickson S6 chains are not safe merge candidates merely because they are numerous or similarly named.
Static observation: The Smoke/GeneratedInterior*/Operational* modules and Structural/CayleyDickson/S6* modules encode phase/proof progression and consumer history. Their boundaries may carry provenance, debugging, or theorem-staging information even if some boilerplate repeats.
Risk: Aggressive consolidation could reduce auditability, hide natural proof boundaries, or create a large monolith that is harder for experts to review.
Refactoring consequence: LEG38 must explicitly classify these as no-merge-until-proven unless a canonical proof module preserves provenance and passes import/declaration regression checks.

Finding: The project root had accumulated generated documentation artifacts and historical Markdown reports, obscuring the source/secondary-artifact boundary.
Static observation: Root inspection of the V1.12 package found 36 Markdown reports, 20 PDFs, 20 TeX files, 19 aux files, 22 logs, 8 out files, 11 toc files, and additional latexmk artifacts at maxdepth 1. These files are not Lean sources and are reproducible or archival documentation surfaces.
Risk: Root clutter can make generated-secondary artifacts look like authoritative inputs, increases package size, and weakens the Single-YAML-Master / Lean-primary discipline.
Refactoring consequence: Keep README.md in root; archive historical Markdown reports; remove generated PDF/TeX/LaTeX build products from root; use scripts/generate_full_document.py for disposable full documentation and scripts/clean_generated_root_artifacts.py for cleanup.

Finding: The V1.14 root policy was too narrow because Lean/Lake project-control files must live in the package/project root for reproducible local builds.
Static observation: User supplied CNNA.lean, lakefile.lean, lake-manifest.json and lean-toolchain. The lakefile points the CNNA library to Repository/repo_snapshot, while the manifest/toolchain pin the Lean/mathlib build environment.
Risk: If root cleanup deletes or omits these files, the package remains documentable but loses its direct local Lake build entry and reproducibility surface.
Refactoring consequence: Amend DBR8/GNG27 by DBR10/GNG28: root is source/control oriented, not README-only. Keep package-root Lean/Lake build files; continue excluding generated PDF/TeX/build artifacts and historical reports from active root.

Finding: Root license/credit and duplicate-README surface needed explicit closure
Static observation: V1.16 had README copies in both package root and Repository/, and no root LICENSE file. User required root-only README, root license file, and explicit editor/assistant credits also in generated PDFs.
Risk: Without closure, release packages could present ambiguous entry surfaces and generated PDFs could lose attribution/license context.
Refactoring consequence: V1.17 keeps README.md and LICENSE only in package root, removes duplicate README files, updates PDF generators and metadata credits, and records GNG29/DBR11.

Finding: Generated documentation sections needed stronger visual separation for review navigation
Static observation: V1.17 full and context PDFs rendered consecutive top-level sections without a uniform automatic pagebreak invariant; individual manual clearpages existed only around the table of contents or specific generator positions.
Risk: Large review packets become harder to navigate and section boundaries can be visually blurred, especially in context-filtered documents that preserve all major sections but show sparse rows.
Refactoring consequence: V1.18 adds a generator-level section hook so every top-level full/context documentation section starts on a fresh page. The change is layout-only and does not alter Lean, YAML row semantics, context filtering, or proof status.

Finding: White phase status was too permissive for unreleased Lean-derivation phases
Static observation: V1.18 had many W phases with implementation_scratchpad.phase_type = derivation, so W still meant unchecked/future rather than non-Lean/tooling-only. User required unreleased Lean-derivation phases to default red until their release is proven from derived-only Lean objects.
Risk: Without this policy, a future Lean-content phase can look merely inactive/white instead of explicitly locked, weakening the derived-only partial-order discipline and hiding missing prerequisite-object evidence.
Refactoring consequence: V1.19 converts all W derivation phases to R, redefines W for non-direct-Lean work, and patches generator validation to reject any future W derivation phase.

Finding: Implementation module manifest could be misread as a second phase table and section/table purposes were underexplained
Static observation: V1.19 still presented the module manifest in the main reading flow and several sections relied on terse table-local paragraphs. User reported confusion and requested clearer static explanations with dynamic table contents only.
Risk: Readers may confuse module/API change planning with phase-release logic, or miss where data comes from and why a table is shown.
Refactoring consequence: V1.20 renames and moves the manifest to a technical appendix, clarifies its use boundary, and adds static source/purpose explanations around the full and context documentation sections.

Finding: Pre-code readiness was too broad: semantic consolidation alone did not prove that all current modules were neutralized, refactored and classified before new substantive Lean code.
Static observation: V1.20 had active 1.1.1.1.5 and orange 1.1.1.2, while many required neutralization/refactor/object-intake phases remained white planning/refactor rows. The codebase still contains phase-semantic SM*/S6*/StrengtheningPhase names and 8860 declarations needing stable classification.
Risk: If 1.1.1.2 starts too early, blue-object correction may be built on unstable module names, incomplete IST-object intake, stale public/API surfaces or unclassified proof-stage modules.
Refactoring consequence: V1.21 splits 1.1.1.1.5 into a five-step readiness chain and makes 1.1.1.2 depend on the final release certificate.

Finding: Database/public-web vision needed explicit non-truth-source planning phases before any direct DB work.
Static observation: SLTG-06 already described database-ready documentation, but provider-neutral SQL database public read-only mirror, snapshot provenance, read-only access policy/views and cnna-project.net frontend contract were not yet split into concrete planning phases.
Risk: Without explicit phases and guardrails, a future database could become an untracked second truth system, leak write authority, lose snapshot provenance or expose admin write credential secrets to browser/frontend contexts.
Refactoring consequence: V1.22 adds 20.15-20.20 plus DBR15-DBR20 and GNG34 as a white non-blocking DB/tooling sidechain. Lean/YAML remain authoritative and active Lean/content cursor is unchanged.

Finding: Generated PDFs needed a larger typography profile for review readability
Static observation: V1.22 used a 10pt document class for full/context PDFs and many explicit scriptsize/footnotesize table fragments, which made large review packets dense even after section-pagebreak and explanation improvements.
Risk: Reviewers may miss status/provenance details in dense tables; readability pressure could otherwise lead to manual PDF edits or inconsistent per-table hacks.
Refactoring consequence: V1.23 applies a one-step font-size increase at generator level: documentclass 11pt, TeX size-command bump in tables, and graph label-size bump where generated by the tool.

Finding: Quaternion/geometric-algebra material needed reference-gate routing before it could influence CNNA planning.
Static observation: Horn 0709.2238v1 contains useful Hamilton/Pauli/Clifford/duality/biquaternion target information, but using it directly would blur CNNA-derived-only provenance and sign/unit semantics.
Risk: Without explicit gates, external Pauli/Clifford/Dirac or biquaternion structures could silently become inputs, set orientation or scalar layers, or patch later Pillar-B physics from established theory.
Refactoring consequence: V1.25 adds GA0-GA6 and comparison phases; GNG37 forbids generator/handoff rights and keeps all material as target/scratchpad context only.

Finding: Phase/object status could be misread as context/type identity; tool-only, database, generated-artifact, governance and reference-gate objects needed explicit separation from Lean objects.
Static observation: Earlier generated phase/object tables showed status and links but did not render an explicit class field on all phase/object logic surfaces.
Risk: Category error: green generated or reference objects could be mistaken for Lean-derived objects; red/yellow Lean phases could be conflated with non-Lean tooling phases.
Refactoring consequence: Add Context class taxonomy and mandatory field, render in phase/object/proof/scratchpad/module-manifest tables, and enforce status/context orthogonality via GNG38.

Finding: Versioning still mixed Lean truth state, YAML planning state and tool-infrastructure state.
Static observation: V1.28 separated tool/display version from Lean-data timestamp, but a planning-only YAML change and a generator-code change could still both appear mainly through the tool version, while phase/object/scratchpad/proof-dossier state had no independent planning-data version.
Risk: Reviewers could misread a plan-table update as a tool-code release, or a generator-code change as a Lean-code/data change. Snapshot provenance for YAML2SQL and the CNNA Explorer would also be ambiguous.
Refactoring consequence: V1.29 introduced three independent metadata axes; V1.30 tightens them into timestamp-complete pairs: lean_data_version/lean_data_timestamp, planning_data_version/planning_data_timestamp and tool_infrastructure_version/tool_infrastructure_timestamp. Legacy tool_version, data_version, data_timestamp and tool_change_timestamp are retained only as compatibility aliases.

Finding: Version labels alone are insufficient for reliable history, SQL snapshots, Explorer provenance and generated artifact review.
Static observation: After V1.29 the three axes existed, but downstream prose and some snapshot contracts could still emphasize the version value without always requiring the paired timestamp at every persistence boundary.
Risk: History becomes incomplete or ambiguous when a planning/tool/Lean version is stored, displayed, imported or compared without the exact timestamp of that axis. A later YAML2SQL snapshot or Explorer diff could then merge distinct states or fail to reconstruct change order.
Refactoring consequence: V1.30 defines every semantic version axis as a mandatory version+timestamp pair, updates the snapshot/provenance wording, and makes the generators validate all six metadata fields: lean_data_version, lean_data_timestamp, planning_data_version, planning_data_timestamp, tool_infrastructure_version and tool_infrastructure_timestamp.

Finding: Lean-axis version and timestamp were still too similar and could be mistaken for duplicate snapshot identifiers.
Static observation: V1.30 required lean_data_version + lean_data_timestamp, but lean_data_version still contained a timestamp-like value. This made the Lean-axis pair look redundant and obscured the distinction between Lean/toolchain/API level and Lean source/data snapshot time.
Risk: History, SQL snapshots and Explorer provenance could later fail to distinguish a Lean toolchain/API upgrade from a Lean source snapshot change. Reviewers could also assume both Lean-axis fields always move together.
Refactoring consequence: V1.31 sets lean_data_version to the static Lean toolchain value lean-4.28.0, keeps lean_data_timestamp at the frozen Lean source snapshot time, and updates generator labels/prose so the Lean axis is rendered as Lean toolchain version + Lean data timestamp.

Finding: Object lifecycle/provenance gap: patched, renamed or deleted objects could previously be overwritten or disappear from current object views.
Static observation: V1.32 adds object lifecycle fields, CEX10, non-destructive patch/rename/delete workflows, phase patch-link workflow, and generator rendering for lifecycle metadata.
Risk: Without lifecycle state and successor/current-dossier pointers, Explorer/SQL history could contradict prior provenance, hide old dossiers, or imply that a historical phase became invalid/outdated.
Refactoring consequence: Treat object currency as lifecycle metadata orthogonal to object status. Objects can become superseded/renamed/deleted-retired; phases remain historical events and link to patch/successor phases instead of becoming outdated.

Finding: Declaration consumption records did not guarantee total classification of the 8000+ Lean declarations by syntactic type, semantic role, triviality, documentation requirement, lifecycle and owner bucket.
Static observation: Before P1.33, declaration_consumption_records.generated.tsv had about 8860 rows with consumption/module/public-promotion fields, while only registered CNNA objects had context/significance/dossier/lifecycle fields. This left a gap between source inventory and Explorer-ready declaration semantics.
Risk: The Explorer could display declarations as if fully sorted, or hide/overexpose declarations, while rfl/projection wrappers, legacy markers, public API surfaces, deleted/renamed declarations and fundamental definitional contracts were not uniquely classified.
Refactoring consequence: P1.33 adds CEX11, total declaration classification taxonomy, workflows and guardrails. LEG37/CEX0 closure now requires exact declaration_kind, semantic_role, triviality_tier, documentation policy, lifecycle_state, public_surface_status and owner bucket for every declaration, plus manual override for heuristic labels.

Finding: Original Roadmap v0.0605 contained migration, chain-sheet, de-seeding, ParameterClosure, D/E and Feynman-audit obligations not yet fully represented in V1.33 planning.
Static observation: Roadmap scan found missing or under-specified terms/structures: De-seeding, Kettenblatt, ParameterClosure, Nuclearity, DHR/DR, Feynman matter audit, local covariance/Hadamard/Wick, scattering/IR horizon, meta-audit markers and old-path rollback.
Risk: Without integration, future Explorer/SQL views and phase planning could look coherent while omitting critical legacy-roadmap gate obligations or semantic warnings.
Refactoring consequence: Add roadmap-integration block 22, concrete future phases 13.8/13.9/15.5/15.6/16.5/16.6, objects B07/B08/PC00/D04/DE00/E04/E05/RDV0-RDV6, guardrails GNG46-GNG50 and generic external-source cross-check rendering.

Finding: REALOQS legacy AQFT surface is substantial and must be treated as a first-class migration/reference source, not ignored.
Static observation: Static scan found 270 Lean files, 1727 declarations, 88 PillarB files, 62 PillarB/AQFT files and 596 AQFT declarations in REALOQS v0.0605 source zip.
Risk: Without a ledger, advanced legacy AQFT work could be silently reintroduced or duplicated without provenance.
Refactoring consequence: Add RLC0-RLC8 and block 23 to route legacy AQFT content through migration disposition and CNNA source maps.

Finding: REALOQS AQFT code cannot be ported 1:1 into CNNA.
Static observation: Legacy AQFT uses custom PillarA/BInterfaces, TreeOfCliques examples/configs, noncomputable sections and @[simp] patterns. The BoundaryMatrix derived layer is entirely noncomputable in the scan.
Risk: Direct reuse would violate derived-only, handoff filtering, operational-path and naming-neutrality constraints.
Refactoring consequence: Require migration disposition, A->B dependency inversion, noncomputable isolation and neutral target modules before implementation.

Finding: Legacy HK/GNS/KMS/quasi-local modules provide useful target gates but not CNNA closure.
Static observation: Legacy code contains HK gates, StarAlgebra/State/GNS/KMS/quasi-local/split structures and derived boundary-matrix routes.
Risk: The names may suggest established AQFT closure even when CNNA source prerequisites are absent.
Refactoring consequence: Record target gates and phase order under RLC4-RLC6; force re-derivation from B local net and later C/D prerequisites.

Finding: The legacy declarations need declaration-level crosswalk before Explorer visibility or migration claims.
Static observation: Initial scan captures module/declaration counts but does not assign every legacy declaration to CNNA owner buckets, target gates, reject/defer buckets or lifecycle states.
Risk: Explorer could display legacy nodes as if they were CNNA-derived or current.
Refactoring consequence: Make RLC8 dependent on CEX11 and block legacy overlay completeness until every visible legacy declaration has explicit non-CNNA epistemic status.

Finding: Workflow 7 could be read as setting a phase red whenever the assistant had not produced a green build, conflating absent build evidence with verified build failure.
Static observation: P1.39 Implementation-from-last-green-baseline said otherwise set the attempted phase R, while Workflow 11 correctly says no build means artifact-generated / local Lean build required, not green.
Risk: The automated phase loop could create unnecessary repair phases or red-lock a phase merely because the current artifact package was planning-only or build evidence was missing.
Refactoring consequence: P1.40 narrows red transitions to verified local/CI failure evidence. Missing build evidence stays evidence-pending/build-required and cannot close, red-lock or advance a phase by itself.

Finding: Post-green closeout still required generated tables and PDF compile, which could be misread as mandatory full-PDF regeneration after P1.39 introduced timestamp-bounded delta PDFs.
Static observation: Workflow 10 predates the delta-PDF rule and did not distinguish bounded planning deltas from full release/audit checkpoints.
Risk: The documentation pipeline could reintroduce full-PDF churn and defeat the timestamp-bounded review discipline.
Refactoring consequence: P1.40 rewrites closeout to require regenerated/validated tables and the applicable documentation artifact: delta PDF by default for bounded changes, full PDF only when explicitly requested or release-gated.

Finding: Red-build failure visibility existed as CEX16 but was not explicitly threaded through all downstream API and deployment dependencies.
Static observation: P1.39 added CEX16 and 21.2.5, but 21.3/CEX3 and 21.9/CEX9 still named CEX12-CEX15 in several dependency fields.
Risk: Explorer API/deployment planning could expose green/current views while omitting red failure history or repair-phase publication obligations.
Refactoring consequence: P1.40 adds CEX16 and 21.2.5 to 21.3/CEX3 and 21.9/CEX9 source chains, proof dossiers and scratchpads.
